ending: Winston's rebellion ends in total defeat. Captured with Julia after their hidden room is exposed as a Thought Police trap, he is held in the windowless Ministry of Love, where O'Brien, the man he had trusted as a fellow dissident, personally oversees his torture and re-education. Over long agony Winston is made to surrender not just his resistance but his belief in objective truth, accepting whatever the Party dictates. He clings to one last loyalty, his refusal to betray his love for Julia, and for that he is taken to Room 101 and threatened with his worst fear, a cage of rats brought to his face, until he breaks and begs that Julia be tortured in his place. That betrayal empties him. Released back into ordinary life, he meets Julia once more, and both admit they betrayed each other and can no longer feel what they once did. Winston now passes his days in a haze of gin and Party slogans. The novel closes as he gazes at Big Brother's portrait and feels a surge of love for the regime that destroyed him: the Party has won completely, taking not only his obedience but his heart and mind. The book ends with an appendix on Newspeak, written in the past tense as though describing a period already over.
in_short: In the totalitarian super-state of Oceania, Party member Winston Smith works at the Ministry of Truth rewriting history to match the regime's lies, while the face of Big Brother watches from every screen. Sickened by the surveillance and the manufactured reality, he begins a secret diary, then a forbidden love affair with a bold younger woman, Julia, and the two rent a hidden room where they can briefly live as themselves. Convinced that the Inner Party official O'Brien is a secret dissident, Winston accepts induction into a supposed underground resistance and is given a book explaining how the Party rules purely for power. But it is all a trap: their room was watched, the friendly shopkeeper is a spy, and O'Brien is a loyal Party inquisitor. Winston is imprisoned and tortured in the Ministry of Love until he surrenders his mind, and in Room 101, faced with his greatest fear, he betrays Julia. Broken and remade, he is freed only to find that he now loves Big Brother.

recaps.text: Winston Smith is a low-ranking member of the Party in Oceania, one of three warring super-states, living in a grimy, rationed London under constant surveillance by telescreens and the Thought Police. He works at the Ministry of Truth, where his job is to alter records so that history always confirms whatever the Party currently claims. Big Brother's face watches from every wall, and the daily Two Minutes Hate whips citizens into fury against the traitor Emmanuel Goldstein. Sickened by the lies and by the erasure of the real past, Winston commits an act of rebellion by starting a secret diary, knowing discovery means death. He is drawn to the Inner Party official O'Brien, whom he imagines to be a fellow doubter, and unsettled by a dark-haired young woman he assumes is spying on him. Through his memories and his forbidden writing, the machinery of the state comes into focus: the rewriting of fact, the policing of thought, and the loneliness of a man who suspects he may be the last person left who still values truth.

Winston's quiet rebellion deepens. At work he sees how thoroughly the Party controls reality, even holding in his hands proof that it has rewritten history before duty forces him to destroy it. He talks with his colleague Syme, who cheerfully explains how Newspeak is being pruned to make dissent literally unthinkable, and Winston concludes that so clear-sighted a man is doomed. He comes to believe that hope, if it exists anywhere, lies with the proles, the unregarded working masses who are largely free of the worst surveillance. Wandering their district, he enters a junk shop run by the gentle old Mr. Charrington, buys a beautiful glass paperweight, and is shown a private upstairs room with no telescreen, an intoxicating glimpse of privacy. Leaving, he sees the dark-haired girl nearby and, certain she is following him, is gripped by fear that his crimes are about to be exposed.

The dark-haired girl, Julia, secretly hands Winston a note confessing that she loves him, turning his dread into desperate hope. After careful, coded arrangements they meet in the countryside and begin a passionate, illegal affair, a rebellion of pleasure and private feeling against a Party that tries to abolish both. Julia, unlike Winston, cares nothing for politics or theory; she breaks the rules simply because she wants to live, and she is expert at deception, hiding her disobedience behind a show of fervent loyalty. They rent the room above Charrington's shop as a secret refuge, filling it with small forbidden comforts. There, briefly, they build a fragile private world of intimacy, real coffee and sugar, and stolen ordinary life, even as both understand that people who do what they are doing are always caught in the end, and that their happiness is borrowed against a certain, terrible reckoning.

Winston and Julia are contacted by O'Brien, who receives them at his comfortable Inner Party flat and confirms Winston's long-held hope by claiming to be part of Goldstein's underground Brotherhood. They swear themselves to the resistance and are given a copy of Goldstein's secret book, which lays out how the Party keeps power for its own sake and how the three super-states wage perpetual, unwinnable war to keep their populations poor and controlled. Reading it aloud together in their rented room, Winston feels he finally understands the world. Then everything collapses: a hidden telescreen has been behind a picture on the wall all along, iron voices order them to freeze, and the house is stormed. The kindly Mr. Charrington reveals himself as an agent of the Thought Police. Winston and Julia are seized and dragged away separately, their sanctuary exposed as a trap that had been closing on them from the start.

Winston is imprisoned in the windowless Ministry of Love, where he learns that O'Brien is not a rebel but a loyal servant of the Party who has been studying and manipulating him for years. Under O'Brien's direction he is starved, beaten, and tortured, forced to abandon fact itself and to accept whatever the Party says is true, even that two plus two makes five. The aim is not merely to punish him but to remake his mind so that he genuinely believes and loves what he once hated. When Winston still holds one private loyalty, his love for Julia, he is taken to Room 101 and confronted with his deepest terror, a cage of rats brought toward his face, until he breaks completely and screams for them to be turned on Julia instead, betraying the one thing he had sworn to protect. Emptied and reshaped, he is eventually released. He meets Julia once more, and each admits to having betrayed the other; whatever they felt is gone. In the final image Winston sits in a cafe, gazing up at the poster of Big Brother, his rebellion utterly defeated, at last loving Big Brother.
